items.myth: fit_transform and transform do the same thing.
items.reality: fit_transform learns parameters from the input then returns transformed data; transform only applies existing parameters. On test data you must use transform alone—fit_transform would relearn and leak information.
items.example: On 1,000 test emails, vec.transform(test) reuses training vocabulary; vec.fit_transform(test) rebuilds vocabulary from test-only tokens.

items.myth: TfidfVectorizer and CountVectorizer are interchangeable drop-in replacements.
items.reality: They produce different numeric semantics—raw or binary counts versus TF–IDF weights. Downstream models and interpretability depend on which representation you choose.
items.example: Multinomial NB expects counts; TfidfVectorizer may yield fractional and negative values unsuitable for multinomial likelihoods.

items.myth: HashingVectorizer needs no training, so data leakage is impossible.
items.reality: The hasher is stateless, but you can still leak labels via tuning on test data or by stacking preprocessing that incorrectly fits on all rows. Label leakage and split discipline remain your responsibility.
items.example: GridSearchCV on the full corpus without CV still picks hyperparameters using test labels even if HashingVectorizer has no fit step.

items.myth: max_features=5000 always keeps the 5000 most important words.
items.reality: sklearn keeps the 5,000 most frequent terms in the training corpus (by term frequency across documents), not by predictive power—rare discriminative tokens can be dropped.
items.example: A fraud keyword appearing in 3 of 50,000 training rows may be pruned while common words like "the" survive unless stop_words or min_df filters apply.
